State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444

March 18, 1982


Roy Huey
494 Cumbre Street
Monterey Park, CA  91754

                                    Re:  Telephone Advice Request

Dear Mr. Huey:

     Thank you for your recent telephone request for advice
as to the applicability of Government Code Sections 87400-
87405-1/ to your circumstances. This letter will confirm my
telephone advice to you.

     Until very recently, you have been employed as an auditor
by the Board of Equalization, State of California. You have
now terminated your state service. Over one year ago, you
completed, and filed, a sales tax audit on Company X. They
have now approached you, expressing a desire to employ you
as a consultant.

     Section 87400 states as follows:

          Unless the contrary is stated or clearly appears
     from the context, the definitions set forth in this
     section shall govern the interpretation of this article.

          (a)  "State administrative agency" means every
     state office, department, division, bureau, board and
     commission, but does not include the Legislature, the
     courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government.

          (b)  "State administrative official" means every
     member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
     administrative agency who as part of his or her official
     responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial
     or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical,
     secretarial or ministerial capacity.

___________________________

1/ Hereafter, all statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise specified.

Roy Huey
March 18, 1982
Page Two


(c) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding"
means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling
or other determination, contract, claim, controversy,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particular matter involving a specific party or parties
in any court or state administrative agency, including
but not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5
(commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of Title 2
of the Government Code.

(d) "Participated" means to have taken part personally
and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a
substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential
information as an officer or employee, but excluding
approval, disapproval or rendering of legal advisory
opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not
involve a specific party or parties. (Emphasis added.)

It seems clear that an audit performed for the Board of
Equalization qualifies as a proceeding and that you were a
state administrative official who participated in that proceeding.

Sections 87401 and 87402 provide the prohibitions, as
follows:

No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office,
shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or
otherwise represent, any other person (other than the
State of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof:

(a) By making any formal or informal appearance,
or by making any oral or written communication with the
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding if;

(b) The State of California is a party or has a
direct and substantial interest; and

(c) If the proceeding is one in which the former
state administrative official participated. (Emphasis
added.)

Section 87401.

Roy Huey
March 18, 1982
Page Three


No former state administrative official, after the
termination of his or her employment or term of office
shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel, consult or
assist in representing any other person (except the
State of California) in any proceeding in which the
official would be prohibited from appearing under Section
87401. (Emphasis added.)

Section 87402.

Consequently, you may not, for compensation, represent
or consult for this company relative to the audit in which
you participated. Apparently, if such an audit is contested,
the appeal process can take some time, perhaps years. However,
you may do other compensated, consulting work for the company
so long as it in no way involves the audit which you worked
on.

If you have further questions, please do not hesitate
to call me, my number is (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

March 18, 1982


Roy Huey
494 Cumbre Street
Monterey Park, CA  91754

                                          Re:  Telephone Advice Request

Dear Mr. Huey:

     Thank you for your recent telephone request for advice
as to the applicability of Government Code Sections 87400-
87405-1/ to your circumstances.  This letter will confirm my
telephone advice to you.

     Until very recently, you have been employed as an auditor
by the Board of Equalization, State of California.  You have
now terminated your state service.  Over one year ago, you
completed and filed, a sales tax audit on Company X.  They
have now approached you, expressing a desire to employ you
as a consultant.

     Section 87400 states as follows:

          Unless the contrary is stated or clearly appears
     from the context, the definitions set forth in this
     section shall govern the interpretation of this article.

          (a)  "State administrative agency" means every
     state office, department, division, bureau, board and
     commission, but does not include the Legislature, the
     courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government.

          (b)  "State administrative official" means every
     member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
     administrative agency who as part of his or her official
     responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial
     or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical,
     secretarial or ministerial capacity.


     1/ Hereafter, all statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise specified.

Memorandum

To : Commissioners Japan, Lowenstein,
McAndrews, Quinn and Remcho

Date : February 9, 197

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
John Giansello

Subject: Revolving Door Legislation

Attached is a first draft of proposed legislation to
restrict the activities of former state officers and employees.
The proposal is presented as a suggested addition to the
conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act.
A few incidental modifications to present Political Reform
Act provisions are also indicated.

The scope of application of the proposed restrictions
is limited in three significant aspects throughout the bill.
First, the proposed bill would regulate only former officers
and employees of the state government. No local or regional
government agencies would be affected. Second, restrictions
and disabilities would be imposed only upon former executive
branch officers and employees. The disabilities and restrictions would not apply to former officers or employees of the
legislative or judicial branches of government. Third, the
disabilities and restrictions apply only to post-employment
relationships in which the former government officer or
employee engages for compensation.

The provisions of this proposed bill attempt to embody
three broad principles of policy:

1. The interests of the government and the public
as a former client should be protected by preventing the disclosure or use of matters revealed by
reason of a relationship having confidential aspects.

2. The integrity of the governmental decision-
making process should be protected by insuring
avoidance of the appearance of impropriety.

3. Both the interests of the government and the
public and the integrity of the governmental decision-
making process should be protected by limitations
on the opportunity for undue advantage by reason
of familiarity with agency practice and procedure
and personal relationships with agency personnel.

Commissioners
February 9, 1979
Page Two


Against these principles must be balanced:

     1.  The ability of the government to recruit com-
petent personnel whose career objectives will
require an assessment of the disadvantages of
government service.

     2.  The advantage to efficient government opera-
tions of having participants in governmental pro-
ceedings who are acquainted with practice, procedure
and precedent.

     3.  The right of private parties to obtain competent
representatives of their own choice.

SECTION BY SECTION ANALYSIS OF THE PROPOSAL

     Section 87400 of the proposed bill defines terms used
throughout. Some of these definitions differ in part from
the definitions of the same terms applicable by Chapter 2 to
the remainder of the Political Reform Act. Except as stated
in Section 87400 or as implied by the context of the proposed
article, the definitions contained in Chapter 2 would also
apply to the proposed article.

     Sections 87401, 87402, 87403 and 87404 are the principal
substantive provisions of the proposed bill.

     Section 87401 prohibits representation of a post-government
employment client in any particular matter, including a
judicial case, an administrative adjudicatory proceeding or
a contractual relationship, in which the former administrative
official participated personally and substantially as a
government official.

     Section 87402, applicable only to situations regulated
by Section 37401, also prohibits a former administrative
official from aiding, assisting or advising anyone else in
representing a client in a matter in which the former adminis-
trative official was personally and substantially involved
as a government official and in which by reason of Section
87401 he would himself be unable to serve as paid representative.

Commissioners
February 9, 1979
Page Three


     Sections 87403 and 87404 regulate post employment activity
when the prior government participation at issue is rulemaking
or development of legislation rather than other particular
matters covered by Sections 87401 and 87402.

     Section 87403 restricts a former administrative official's
paid representation of private clients in administrative
rulemaking or legislative proceedings in which the former
official participated personally and substantially as a
government employee.

     Section 87404 restricts such an official's paid representa-
tion of private clients in judicial, administrative adjudicatory
or contract proceedings in which the validity, interpretation
or application of rules or legislation is at issue if the
former official participated personally and substantially in
the particular rulemaking or legislative proceeding involved
as a government employee.

     Both Sections 87403 and 87404 impose the respective
disabilities upon the former administrative official for a
period of one year only following termination of government
employment. The prohibitions relating to particular matters
in Sections 87401 and 87402, on the other hand, are permanent.

     Section 87405 provides for three exceptions to the
regulatory provisions of the article. A former administrative
official would not be prevented from prosecuting matters of
a personal and individual nature in his own behalf, from
receiving the normal fees if subpoenaed as a witness, and
from communicating scientific or technological information
if the agency or court to which the communication is addressed
makes findings that the former official has outstanding and
otherwise unavailable qualifications, that the matter at
issue requires such expertise and that the public interest
would be served thereby.

     Section 87406 makes clear that the administrative advisory
opinion, investigation and hearing provisions granted to the
Commission in Chapter 3 of the Political Reform Act apply
also to the proposed article. Additionally, this section
adds another administrative remedy to those already available
in Section 83116. Anyone found administratively to have
violated the proposed article could be barred, at the Commission's
option, from paid representation of any person on any matter
before the agency of former employment or in any matter in
which that agency has direct and substantial interest, for a
period of three years.

Commissioners
February 9, 1979
Page Four


     Section 87407 empowers the judge, presiding officer or
other person in charge of a pending proceeding, upon the
petition of an interested person and after notice and oppor-
tunity for a hearing, to exclude anyone found to be in vio-
lation of the proposed article from further participation in
the pending proceeding.

     Section 87408 imposes the requirements of the proposed
article only prospectively, so that only persons assuming
state office or employment after the effective date of the
article would be covered thereby.

     Sections two, three and four of the proposed bill amend
three sections of the present Political Reform Act to harmonize
them with the requirements and language of the new article.
Of the three, only section four is of substantive significance.
It would allow the county court to set aside an official
action if the court finds that a violation of the new article
has occurred, that the official action might not otherwise
have been or approved, and that the person represented
by a former administrative official in violation of the new
article knew or should have known that such representation
was prohibited, with the exception of the third of these
required findings, this remedy is similar to that presently
provided for violation of Article 1 of Chapter 7 of the
Political Reform Act.

JG:kp

ASSEMBLY COMMITTEE ON ELECTIONS AND REAPPORTIONMENT
MARILYN RYAN, Chairman
Room 2196
445-5430

BRUCE BOLINGER, Consultant


BILL DIGEST


BILL:         AB 1048

AUTHOR:       Assemblywoman Maxine Waters

SUBJECT:      Conflict of interest of former state executive
              officers and employees.


BACKGROUND:

     The Political Reform Act prohibits conflict of interest
on the part of any public official in state or local govern-
ment and requires every state and local government agency to
adopt a conflict of interest code. However, it does not im-
pose any restrictions on the activity of former state employees
or former holders of state office.


DESCRIPTION:

     With certain exceptions, the measure prohibits any
former state agency official after the termination of his
employment or term of office from representing for compen-
sation any other person in connection with a judicial, quasi-
judicial, or other proceeding in which (1) the person repre-
sented is a party, (2) the State of California is a party
or has a direct and substantial interest, and (3) the former
agency official participated. Nor shall any former state
agency official for compensation aid, advise, assist, etc.
any person in such circumstances.

     Anyone found in violation of the prohibition could be

                                                    (CONTINUED)

AB 1048
Page 2

excluded by a court or presiding or other officer from further
participation or from assisting or counseling any other participant in a pending proceeding.

The measure will not apply to anyone who has left government
service prior to its effective date.


COMMENT:

1. The measure is supported by the FPPC.

2. An FPPC staff analysis of the legislation notes
that the interests of the government and the public as a
former client and the integrity of the governmental decision-
making process should be protected -
     (1) by preventing the disclosure or use of matters re-
vealed by reason of a relationship having confidential aspects,
(2) by reducing the chance that post-government employment
considerations will bias the conduct of government business, and
(3) by imposing limitations on the opportunity for undue
advantage by reason of familiarity with agency practice and
procedure and personal relationships with agency personnel.

It noted, on the other hand, (1) that the government
must be able to recruit competent personnel whose career
objectives will require an assessment of the disadvantages
of government service, (2) that there is an advantage to
efficient government operations of having participants in
governmental proceedings who are acquainted with practice,
procedure, and precedent, and (3) that private parties have
the right to obtain competent representatives of their own
choice.

Legislative .lyst
June 6, 1979

ANALYSIS OF ASSEMBLY BILL NO. 1048 (Maxine Waters)

1979-80 Session

Fiscal Effect:

Cost:     No added state cost.

Revenue:  None.

Analysis:

This bill imposes various restrictions on the activi-
ties of former state employees concerning their subsequent
employment for compensation in connection with specified
judicial or other proceedings. The bill applies only to per-
sons who leave government service after the effective date
of the bill.

The Fair Political Practices Commission states that
the bill would involve minor additional workload which could
be met by existing staff.

8185

AIIS AND MEANS STAFF ANALYSIS

BILL NUMBER AB 1048_____ AUTHOR____M. Waters_______ AMENDED__-0-__ ITEM_54

INDEX Elections     POLICY COMMITTEE Elec. & Reap.     CONSULTANT Sharpless

SUBJECT: Conflict of interest of former state executive officers and employees.

FISCAL SUMMARY:                    FUND        1978/79 FY    1979/80 FY    1980/01 FY
                                  (G,S,N,D,
                                   F, or L)

State Cost:
FPPC respond to inquiries          G           ___________   Insignificant  Insignificant

Urgency: Yes // No //

SUMMARY:
AB 1048 prohibits, except in specified instances, any former state executive officer or
employee from representing for compensation any other person in a judicial, quasi-judicial
or other proceeding in which 1) the person represented is a party 2) the state is a
party or has a direct or substantial interest or 3) the former agency official partici-
pated.

The bill would not affect anyone who has left state government service prior to the
date of the effectiveness.

COMMENTS:
1. This bill would not affect officials or employees from the legislative or judicial
   branches of government.

2. AB 1048 is supported by the Fair Political Practices Commission.

3. The FPPC indicates that they have been few examples of the type of conflict of inter-
   est which would be prohibited by this bill.

FISCAL IMPACT:
The Fair Political Practices Commission anticipates only insignificant costs to handle
inquiries regarding the act's application.

ANALYSIS:
There is currently no law which prohibits conflict of interest activities on the part of
former state employees or state officials.

This bill prohibits any former state agency official after the termination of his employ-
ment or term of office from representing for compensation any other person in connection
with a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceedings in which 1) the person represented is
party 2) the state is a party or has a direct and substantial interest and 3) the
former agency official participated.

The bill also prohibits a former state executive employee or official from aiding, advising, assisting, etc. any such person in such circumstances for compensation.

Former agency officials would be exempt from the prohibition if:

1) presenting or providing testimony as an expert witness for no compensation other
   than that provided by law or regulation for witnesses.

AB 1048                                                    Page 2
Assemblywoman Waters


2)  communications with state agencies to provide information after the agency makes
    a finding in writing that the former official 1) has outstanding or otherwise
    unavailable qualifications  2) is acting with respect to a particular matter
    which requires such qualifications, and 3) the public interest would be served.

The bill would not affect anyone who has left state government service prior to the date of
effectiveness.

Anyone found in violation of the prohibition could be excluded by a court or presiding or
other officer from further participation or from assisting or counseling any other participant in a pending proceeding.

Senate Committee on
Governmental Organization
Hearing Date: 7/17/79

STAFF ANALYSIS OF ASSEMBLY BILL NO. 1048 (Waters, Maxine)
(As Amended July 11, 1979)                                A
                                                          B

SUBJECT

Conflict of interest;                          1
restricted activities of former                0
state administrative officials.                4
                                               B

DESCRIPTION

1.    AB 1048 would prohibit any former state administra-
      tive official, after termination of his/her employ-
      ment or term of office, from representing for compensa-
      tion any other person (other than the State of
      California) in connection with a judicial, quasi-
      judicial or other proceeding in which a) the State
      of California is a party or has a direct and sub-
      stantial interest, and b) the proceeding is one in
      which the former state administrative official
      participated.

2.    Defines a number of terms such as "state administra-
      tive agency," "state administrative official,"
      "judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding," "partici-
      pated" and "state agency."

3.    The provisions of this bill DO NOT apply to the
      Legislature, the courts, or any agency in the
      judicial branch of government.

4.    Prohibits a former state administrative official
      from aiding, advising, counseling, consulting or
      assisting in representing any person for compensa-
      tion, but waives these prohibitions if:

                          - MORE -

AB 1048 (Wate. , Maxine) - continued                    Page 2

     a.  The former official is making a statement
         based on special knowledge in the particular
         area, provided that no compensation is received
         other than that regularly provided for by law
         or regulation for a witness.

     b.  The agency to which the former official is
         communicating makes written findings that:

              (1)  The former official has outstanding
                   qualifications otherwise unavailable.

              (2)  He/she is acting with respect to a
                   particular matter which requires these
                   qualifications.

              (3)  The public interest would be served by
                   his or her participation.

     c.  At least 5 years have elapsed since the termina-
         tion of the former officials employment or term
         of office; and the public interest would not be
         harmed.

     5.  The measure states that anyone found in violation
         of the prohibition can be excluded by a court or
         other officer from further participation, or from
         assisting or counseling any other participant in
         a pending proceeding.

     6.  AB 1048 does not affect anyone who has left state
         government prior to the bill's effective date.
         However, anyone returning to state service after
         this measure is enacted will be covered.


                              - MORE -

AB 1048 (Water., Maine) - continued                    Page 3

BACKGROUND

This measure is sponsored by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.

Under current law, the Political Reform Act does not
provide restrictions on the activities of officers and
employees who formerly served in the executive branch
of state government and who subsequently engage in
post-government employment for compensation.

Sponsors of AB 1048 feel that the interest of state
government and the public can best be protected by:
1. preventing the disclosure or use of matters
   revealed by reason of a relationship having
   confidential aspects;
2. reducing the chance that post-government
   employment considerations will bias the conduct
   of government business; and
3. imposing limitations on the opportunity for
   undue advantage by reason of familiarity with
   agency practice and procedure, and personal
   relationships with agency personnel.

Opponents believe that attorneys should be excluded from
the provisions of AB 1048, in that they are covered by
the ethics code and supervision of the California Bar
Association.

KNOWN POSITIONS:

The following SUPPORT AB 1048:
   California State Employees Association
   Fair Political Practices Commission

The following OPPOSE AB 1048:
   Association of California State Attorneys

WRITTEN COMMUNICATIONS RECEIVED:  None

FISCAL COMMITTEE:  Senate Finance Committee

State of California

Memorandum

Department of Justice

To : Herbert W. Nobriga, Director
Office of Administrative Hearings
717 K Street, Suite 409
Sacramento, CA 95814

Date : December 30, 1980

File No.:

From : Office of the Attorney General

DEC 31 1980

Subject: ANALYSIS OF CHAPTER 66, STATUTES OF 1980

Your request for informal advice relating to the effect
of Chapter 66, Statutes of 1980, upon employees of the Office of
Administrative Hearings has been referred to me for response.
What follows is my general analysis as to whom and in what circum-
stances the prohibitions of Chapter 66 apply. Before commencing
such an analysis, however, I must caution that this memorandum is
not an opinion of the Office of the Attorney General and should
not be cited or relied upon as such.

Chapter 66 prohibits designated activity in conjunction
with judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings by former
state administrative officials who participated in such proceed-
ings while in state service. (See Govt. Code §§ 87401 & 87402)
Some understanding of the extent and application of these prohi-
bitions can be gleaned from the statutory definitions of their
critical terms.

The prohibitions apply only to former "state administra-
tive officials". This term is defined in Government Code Section
87400, subdivision (b), to include persons who, as part of their
official responsibility, engage in judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceedings in other than a purely clerical, secretarial, or
ministerial fashion. The question of whether a particular indivi-
dual is or was so engaged as part of his or her official responsi-
bilities necessitates a factual inquiry well beyond the scope of
this memorandum. However, with regard to the positions identified
in your memorandum, one can reasonably conclude that a hearing
reporter and an executive secretary typically are not state adminis-
trative officials.

If a former state administrative official is involved
it must next be determined if a "judicial, quasi-judicial or other
proceeding" existed in which the official "participated". The
term "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" is defined in
Government Code Section 87400, subdivision (c). Pertinent to
your apparent concerns is the language limiting the term to parti-

cular matters "involving a specific party or parties in any court
or state administrative agency". Thus, unless a matter focuses
upon a specific party or particles, it is not a judicial, quasi-
judicial, or other proceeding within the meaning of Chapter 66,
and participation therein is of no legal significance. The term
"specific party or parties" is not defined in Chapter 66, and I
hesitate to speculate as to how it may hereafter be interpreted
by the courts or this office.

If a former administrative official is involved and if
the judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding existed, the
remaining inquiry is whether the official "participated" in the
proceeding while in state service. The term "participated" is
defined in Government Code Section 8740, subdivision (C). It is
noteworthy that participation is defined to require personal and
substantial involvement by the state administrative official in
the judicial, quasi judicial, or other proceeding. The question
of whether a particular individual was personally and substantially
involved in such a proceeding requires a factual determination
also beyond the scope of this memorandum.

If the prohibitions apply, the only time limitation upon
their duration is set forth in Government Code Section 87403,
subdivision (c). This prohibition relates to proceedings in which
the state administrative agency has rendered a final decision,
but has also retained jurisdiction over the matter. In such
instances, the former state administrative official is exempted
from the prohibitions upon a finding by the agency that five (5)
years have elapsed since the official's separation from state
service and that the public interest would not be harmed by his
or her involvement in the proceeding.

It is my hope that the above analysis will provide some
counsel to you and other concerned Office of Administrative Hearings
employees. However, I must reiterate that the above reflects
solely the views of the undersigned and that this memorandum should
not be cited or relied upon as an official opinion of the Office
of the Attorney General.

If you have any further questions or if you otherwise
desire to discuss this matter, please feel free to telephone me at
445-8208.

M. Anthony Soares
Deputy Attorney General

The bill would not affect anyone who has left state government service prior to the date of the effectiveness.

COMMENTS:
1. This bill would not affect officials or employees from the legislative or judicial branches of government.

2. AB 1048 is supported by the 

Date: December 10, 1980

From: Office of the Attorney General

Subject: ANALYSIS OF CHAPTER 66, STATUTES OF 1980

Your request for informal advice relating to the effect of Chapter 66, Statutes of 1980, upon employees of the Office of Administrative Hearings has been referred to me for response. What follows is my general analysis as to whom and in what circumstances the prohibitions of Chapter 66 apply. Before commencing such an analysis, however, I must caution that this memorandum is not an opinion of the Office of the Attorney General and should not be cited or relied upon as such.

Chapter 66 prohibits designated activity in conjunction with judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings by former state administrative officials who participated in such proceedings while in state service. (See Govt. Code §§ 87401 & 87402) Some understanding of the extent and application of these prohibitions can be gleaned from the statutory definitions of their critical terms.

The prohibitions apply only to former "state administrative officials". This term is defined in Government Code Section 87400, subdivision (b), to include persons who, as part of their official responsibility, engage in judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings in other than a purely clerical, secretarial, or ministerial fashion. The question of whether a particular individual is or was so engaged as part of his or her official responsibilities necessitates a factual inquiry well beyond the scope of this memorandum. However, with regard to the positions identified in your memorandum, one can reasonably conclude that a hearing reporter and an executive secretary typically are not state administrative officials.

If a former state administrative official is involved, it must next be determined if a "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" existed in which the official "participated". The term "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" is defined in Government Code Section 87400, subdivision (c). Pertinent to your apparent concerns is the language limiting the term to parti

cular matters "involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency." Thus, unless the matter focuses upon a specific party or parties, it is not a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding within the meaning of Chapter 66, and participation therein is of no legal significance. The term "specific party or parties" is not defined in Chapter 66, and I hesitate to speculate as to how it may hereafter be interpreted by the courts or this office.

If a former administrative official is involved and if the judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding existed, the remaining inquiry is whether the official "participated" in the proceeding while in state service. The term "participated" is defined in Government Code Section 87400, subdivision (d). It is noteworthy that participation is defined to require personal and substantial involvement by the state administrative official in the judicial, quasi, judicial, or other proceeding. The question of whether a particular individual was personally and substantially involved in such a proceeding requires a factual determination also beyond the scope of this memorandum.

If the prohibitions apply, the only time limitation upon their duration is set forth in Government Code Section 87403, subdivision (c). This prohibition relates to proceedings in which the state administrative agency has rendered a final decision, but has also retained jurisdiction over the matter. In such instances, the former state administrative official is exempted from the prohibitions upon a finding by the agency that five (5) years have elapsed since the official's separation from state service and that the public interest would not be harmed by his or her involvement in the proceeding.

It is my hope that the above analysis will provide some counsel to you and other concerned Office of Administrative Hearings employees. However, I must reiterate that the above reflects solely the views of the undersigned and that this memorandum should not be cited or relied upon as an official opinion of the Office of the Attorney General.
sections.facts:
The Political Reform Act prohibits conflict of interest on the part of any public official in state or local government and requires every state and local government agency to adopt a conflict of interest code. However, it does not impose any restrictions on the activity of former state employees or former holders of state office.

DESCRIPTION:

With certain exceptions, the measure prohibits any former state agency official after the termination of his employment or term of office from representing for compensation any other person in connection with a judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which (1) the person represented is a party, (2) the State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest, and (3) the former agency official participated. Nor shall any former state agency official for compensation aid, advise, assist, etc. any person in such circumstances.

Anyone found in violation of the prohibition could be

excluded by a court or presiding or other officer from further participation or from assisting or counseling any other participant in a pending proceeding.

The measure will not apply to anyone who has left government service prior to its effective date.

COMMENT:

1. The measure is supported by the FPPC.

2. An FPPC staff analysis of the legislation notes that the interests of the government and the public as a former client and the integrity of the governmental decision-making process should be protected -
   (1) by preventing the disclosure or use of matters revealed by reason of a relationship having confidential aspects,
   (2) by reducing the chance that post-government employment considerations will bias the conduct of government business, and
   (3) by imposing limitations on the opportunity for undue advantage by reason of familiarity with agency practice and procedure and personal relationships with agency personnel.

It noted, on the other hand, (1) that the government must be able to recruit competent personnel whose career objectives will require an assessment of the disadvantages of government service, (2) that there is an advantage to efficient government operations of having participants in governmental proceedings who are acquainted with practice, procedure, and precedent, and (3) that private parties have the right to obtain competent representatives of their own choice.

Legislative Analyst  
June 6, 1979

ANALYSIS OF ASSEMBLY BILL NO. 1048 (Maxine Waters)
1979-80 Session

Fiscal Effect:

Cost: No added state cost.

Revenue: None.
sections.analysis:
This bill imposes various restrictions on the activities of former state employees concerning their subsequent employment for compensation in connection with specified judicial or other proceedings. The bill applies only to persons who leave government service after the effective date of the bill. The Fair Political Practices Commission states that the bill would involve minor additional workload which could be met by existing staff. **AYS AND MEANS STAFF ANALYSIS** BILL NUMBER: AB 1048 AUTHOR: M. Waters AMENDED: -O- ITEM: 54 INDEX: Elections POLICY COMMITTEE: Elec. & Reap. CONSULTANT: Sharpless SUBJECT: Conflict of interest of former state executive officers and employees. FISCAL SUMMARY:
FUND 1978/79 FY 1979/80 FY 1980/81 FY
(G, S, N, B, F, or L) Insignificant Insignificant
State Cost: FPPC respond to inquiries: G Urgency: Yes / No /
sections.question_synthetic: Can a former state auditor for the Board of Equalization accept compensated consulting work from a company that was the subject of a sales tax audit the auditor previously completed?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A former state auditor is permanently prohibited from being compensated to represent or consult for a client regarding an audit in which the official personally and substantially participated, though the former official may perform other compensated consulting work for that client on unrelated matters.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document contains two versions of the advice letter (appears to be duplicate or scanning artifact). Also includes partial draft legislative analysis on revolving door legislation which is tangential. Extracted primary advice letter dated March 18, 1982 from Robert E. Leidigh. Clear question and conclusion sections present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Until very recently, you have been employed as an auditor by the Board of Equalization, State of California. You have now terminated your state service. Over one year ago, you completed, and filed, a sales tax audit on Company X. They have now approached you, expressing a desire to employ you as a consultant. [Applicability of Government Code Sections 87400-87405 to these circumstances]

CONCLUSION: AB 1048 prohibits, except in specified instances, any former state executive officer or employee from representing for compensation any other person in a judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in which 1) the person represented is a party 2) the state is a party or has a direct or substantial interest or 3) the former agency official participated.

The bill would not affect anyone who has left state government service prior to the date of the effectiveness.

COMMENTS:
1. This bill would not affect officials or employees from the legislative or judicial branches of government.

2. AB 1048 is supported by the

embedding.first_500_words: March 18, 1982 Roy Huey 494 Cumbre Street Monterey Park, CA 91754 Re: Telephone Advice Request Dear Mr. Huey: Thank you for your recent telephone request for advice as to the applicability of Government Code Sections 87400-87405 1/ to your circumstances. This letter will confirm my telephone advice to you. Until very recently, you have been employed as an auditor by the Board of Equalization, State of California. You have now terminated your state service. Over one year ago, you completed, and filed, a sales tax audit on Company X. They have now approached you, expressing a desire to employ you as a consultant. Section 87400 states as follows: Unless the contrary is stated or clearly appears from the context, the definitions set forth in this section shall govern the interpretation of this article. (a) "State administrative agency" means every state office, department, division, bureau, board and commission, but does not include the Legislature, the courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government. (b) "State administrative official" means every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part of his or her official responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity. 1/ Hereafter, all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified. (c) "Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency, including but not limited to any proceeding governed by Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code. (d) "Participated" means to have taken part personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of confidential information as an officer or employee, but excluding approval, disapproval or rendering of legal advisory opinions to departmental or agency staff which do not involve a specific party or parties. (Emphasis added.) It seems clear that an audit performed for the Board of Equalization qualifies as a proceeding and that you were a state administrative official who participated in that proceeding. Sections 87401 and 87402 provide the prohibitions, as follows: No former state administrative official, after the termination of his or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the State of California) before any court or state administrative agency or any officer or employee thereof: (a) By making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding if; (b) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial interest; and (c) If the proceeding is one in which the former state administrative official participated. (Emphasis added.) Section
embedding.summary: FPPC counsel advises a former Board of Equalization auditor that Government Code Sections 87401-87402 prohibit him from accepting compensated representation or consulting work related to a sales tax audit he completed, though he may accept other compensated consulting work from the same company on unrelated matters.
